sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Token at rows 30-32, cols 30-32, blocked by purple wall at cols 33-38. The wall spans full platform height (rows 21-41). Cannot reach right container from current position. Need to reconsider the goal or find an alternate path.
- Plan: Move token LEFT back to platform, then try DOWN toward the left container area to test if stacking or swapping is possible.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see clearly. The left container is at rows 32-36, cols 10-14 (the 'c' border). The left token (N) is inside the left container at rows 33-35, cols 11-13.
But wait, looking at the ascii:
```
Row 33: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
Row 34: gwwcNcNcwwwwwNWNg
Row 35: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
```
There are TWO tokens visible:
1. Left token: rows 33-35, cols 11-13 (inside the left container)
2. Right token: rows 33-35, cols 21-23 (on the platform, outside the container)
Wait, but the right token should have moved from rows 30-32 to rows 33-35 after DOWN. And it should be at cols 21-23 after 3 LEFT moves from cols 30-32. That matches: cols 30-32 -> 27-29 -> 24-26 -> 21-23, and rows 30-32 -> 33-35.
So the right token is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. The left container is at rows 32-36, cols 10-14. The left token is inside the left container at rows 33-35, cols 11-13.
Now, the right token is at cols 21-23, and the left container is at cols 10-14. There's a gap of cols 15-20 between them. If I move the right token LEFT, it goes to cols 18-20, then 15-17, then 12-14. At cols 12-14, the token would be inside the left container (cols 10-14).
But the left container is already occupied by the left token. Can two tokens occupy the same container? Or does the right token need to go to the right container instead?
Actually, looking at the ascii again:
```
Row 33: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
```
The left container is at cols 10-14 ('cNNNc'), and the left token is at cols 11-13 ('NNN'). The right token is at cols 21-23 ('NNN').
Wait, I see two 'NNN' patterns on row 33. One at cols 11-13 (left token in container) and one at cols 21-23 (right token on platform). So there are indeed two tokens.
Now, the goal is to put the right token into the right container. The right container is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48 (the 'c' pattern on the right platform). But the purple wall blocks the path from the left platform to the right platform.
Hmm, but wait. Looking at the ascii, the right container is at:
```
Row 26: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
Row 27: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 28: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 29: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 30: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
```
The right container is at rows 26-30, cols 49-53 (the 'c' pattern). But the purple wall is at cols 33-38. The right platform is at cols 39-50 (white 'w'). So the right container is on the right platform.
To reach the right container from the left platform, the token needs to cross the purple wall. But the wall blocks all paths.
Wait, but maybe I'm wrong about the wall. Let me look at the ascii more carefully:
```
Row 30: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
```
The wall is at cols 33-38 ('pppppp'). The right platform starts at col 39 ('w'). So the wall is between the left and right platforms.
But wait, there's a 'g' at col 32, which is the gray background. So the left platform ends at col 31, and the wall starts at col 33. There's a 1-column gap at col 32.
Actually, looking at the ascii:
```
Row 30: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
         0123456789...
```
Let me count from col 8 (start of my crop):
- Col 8: 'g'
- Cols 9-24: 'wwwwwwwwwwwwwwww' (16 chars) - left platform
- Col 25: 'g' - gray background
- Cols 26-31: 'pppppp' (6 chars) - purple wall
- Cols 32-36: 'wwwww' (5 chars) - right platform
- Cols 37-41: 'ccccc' (5 chars) - right container border
- Col 42: 'w'
Wait, that doesn't match my earlier analysis. Let me recount more carefully.
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw`
Starting from index 0 of the cropped string (which is col 8 of the full grid):
- Index 0 (col 8): 'g'
- Index 1-16 (cols 9-24): 'wwwwwwwwwwwwwwww' (16 chars) - left platform
- Index 17 (col 25): 'g' - gray background
- Index 18-23 (cols 26-31): 'pppppp' (6 chars) - purple wall
- Index 24-28 (cols 32-36): 'wwwww' (5 chars) - right platform
- Index 29-33 (cols 37-41): 'ccccc' (5 chars) - right container border
- Index 34 (col 42): 'w'
Hmm, but earlier I saw the token at cols 21-23 on the left platform. Let me check:
Row 33: `gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg`
- Index 0 (col 8): 'g'
- Index 1-2 (cols 9-10): 'ww'
- Index 3 (col 11): 'c' - left container border
- Index 4-6 (cols 12-14): 'NNN' - left token
- Index 7 (col 15): 'c' - left container border
- Index 8-12 (cols 16-20): 'wwwww' - left platform
- Index 13-15 (cols 21-23): 'NNN' - right token
- Index 16 (col 24): 'g' - gray background
So the right token is at cols 21-23, and the left platform extends from cols 9-24 (with the container at cols 10-15). The right token is on the left platform.
Now, to reach the right container, the token needs to cross the purple wall at cols 26-31. The wall is 6 columns wide, and the token is 3 columns wide. The token can't jump over the wall.
But wait, maybe there's a gap in the wall at some row. Let me check the wall's extent:
```
Row 26: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
Row 27: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 28: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 29: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcwwwcw
Row 30: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
```
The wall is at cols 26-31 in all these rows. So the wall spans rows 26-30 (at least). Let me check other rows.
Actually, from the segmentation earlier, the wall is at [21,33]-[41,38]. But in my ascii crop, the wall appears at cols 26-31. There's a discrepancy of 7 columns. This is because my crop starts at col 8, not col 0. So the wall is at cols 26+8=34 to 31+8=39 in the full grid. But the segmentation says [21,33]-[41,38], which is rows 21-41, cols 33-38. That's close to cols 34-39, but not exact.
I think the discrepancy is due to my cropping. Let me just trust the ascii for now.
OK so the wall is at cols 26-31 (in the cropped view, starting at col 8), which is cols 34-39 in the full grid. The wall spans rows 21-41. The token is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23 (full grid cols 29-31).
Wait, I'm getting confused again. Let me just use the ascii directly.
In the cropped view (starting at col 8):
- Left platform: cols 9-24 (full grid)
- Wall: cols 26-31 (full grid)
- Right platform: cols 32-36 (full grid)
- Right container: cols 37-41 (full grid)
- Right token: cols 21-23 (full grid)
So the right token is at full grid cols 21-23, and the wall is at full grid cols 26-31. The gap between the token and the wall is cols 24-25 (2 columns). The token is 3 columns wide, so if it moves RIGHT, it goes to cols 24-26, which overlaps with the wall at col 26. So the wall blocks.
But wait, the left platform ends at col 24, and the wall starts at col 26. There's a 1-column gap at col 25. If the token moves RIGHT from cols 21-23, it goes to cols 24-26. Col 24 is the edge of the platform, col 25 is the gap, and col 26 is the wall. So the token would partially overlap with the wall.
Hmm, but earlier I moved the token RIGHT from cols 21-23 to cols 24-26, and it worked (the token moved). So maybe the token CAN move into the gap, even if it partially overlaps with the wall.
Actually, looking at the ascii again:
```
Row 30: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgppppppwwwwwcccccw
```
The platform is at cols 9-24, the gap is at col 25 ('g'), and the wall is at cols 26-31. So the platform extends to col 24, and the wall starts at col 26. The gap at col 25 is gray background.
But the token is at cols 21-23 (on the platform). If it moves RIGHT, it goes to cols 24-26. Col 24 is the platform, col 25 is the gap, and col 26 is the wall. So the token would span cols 24-26, which includes the gap and the wall.
Earlier, I moved the token RIGHT and it did move. So the token CAN move into the gap and partially overlap with the wall. But can it move further RIGHT?
Let me try moving the token RIGHT and see if it can pass the wall.
Actually wait, I just realized something. The token is at rows 33-35, but the right container is at rows 26-30. So even if the token can cross the wall, it needs to be at the right row to enter the container.
Let me think about this differently. The right container is at rows 26-30, cols 37-41 (full grid). The token is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. To reach the container, the token needs to:
1. Move RIGHT to cross the wall (cols 26-31)
2. Move UP to reach rows 26-30
But the wall blocks RIGHT movement. And the token is currently at rows 33-35, which is below the container.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the right container more carefully:
```
Row 26: ...wwwwwcccccw
Row 27: ...wwwwwcwwwcw
Row 28: ...wwwwwcwwwcw
Row 29: ...wwwwwcwwwcw
Row 30: ...wwwwwcccccw
```
The container is a 5x5 square (rows 26-30, cols 37-41). The interior is at rows 27-29, cols 38-40 ('www').
So the container is at rows 26-30. The token is at rows 33-35. If the token moves UP, it goes to rows 30-32, then 27-29, then 24-26. At rows 27-29, the token is at the same row range as the container's interior.
But the token is at cols 21-23, and the container is at cols 37-41. The token needs to move RIGHT to reach the container.
OK so the plan is:
1. Move the token UP to rows 27-29 (or higher)
2. Move RIGHT to cross the wall
3. Move RIGHT to reach the container
4. Move DOWN (if needed) to enter the container
But the wall blocks RIGHT movement. Unless there's a gap in the wall at some row.
Let me check the wall's extent more carefully. From the segmentation, the wall is at [21,33]-[41,38]. In the full grid, that's rows 21-41, cols 33-38. But in my ascii crop (starting at col 8), the wall appears at cols 26-31, which corresponds to full grid cols 34-39. There's a 1-column discrepancy.
Actually, I think the segmentation and ascii are consistent, and I'm just miscounting. Let me trust the ascii.
From the ascii, the wall is at cols 26-31 (full grid), rows 21-41. The token is at cols 21-23, rows 33-35. The wall blocks RIGHT movement at all rows from 21 to 41.
So the token can't cross the wall. The only way to reach the right container is if there's a gap in the wall or if the token can somehow bypass it.
Wait, but what if the goal is NOT to put the token in the right container? What if the goal is to put the token in the LEFT container?
Looking at the left container:
```
Row 32: gwwcccccwwwwwwwww
Row 33: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
Row 34: gwwcNcNcwwwwwNWNg
Row 35: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
Row 36: gwwcccccwwwwwwwwg
```
The left container is at rows 32-36, cols 10-14 (full grid). The left token is inside at rows 33-35, cols 11-13.
The right token is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. If I move the right token LEFT, it goes to cols 18-20, then 15-17, then 12-14. At cols 12-14, the token would be at the right edge of the left container.
But the left container is already occupied by the left token. Can two tokens occupy the same container?
Actually, looking at the ascii, the left container's interior is at rows 33-35, cols 11-13 ('NNN/NcN/NNN'). The 'c' in the middle of row 34 suggests the container has a cross-shaped interior or something.
Wait, let me look more carefully:
```
Row 33: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
Row 34: gwwcNcNcwwwwwNWNg
Row 35: gwwcNNNcwwwwwNNNg
```
The left container is:
- Row 32: 'ccccc' (top border)
- Row 33: 'cNNNc' (left border, token, right border)
- Row 34: 'cNcNc' (left border, token middle, center 'c', token right, right border)
- Row 35: 'cNNNc' (left border, token, right border)
- Row 36: 'ccccc' (bottom border)
So the container is 5x5, with a border of 'c' and an interior of 'N' (the token). The center of the container (row 34, col 13) is 'c', which is the container's border crossing.
Wait, that's weird. The container's interior should be empty (not 'c'), but the center is 'c'. Maybe the container has a cross-shaped border?
Actually, looking at the right container:
```
Row 26: ...ccccc
Row 27: ...cwwwc
Row 28: ...cwwwc
Row 29: ...cwwwc
Row 30: ...ccccc
```
The right container has a rectangular border with an empty interior ('www'). So the left container should have the same structure, but it's occupied by the left token.
OK so the left container is occupied, and the right container is empty. The goal is to put the right token in the right container.
But the wall blocks the path. I'm stuck.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to move the token UP first, then RIGHT, to see if there's a gap in the wall at a higher row.
Actually, wait. Let me check if the wall extends to the top of the platform. From the ascii:
```
Row 21: wwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwww
```
The wall ('pppppp') is at rows 21-41. The platform starts at row 21. So the wall spans the full height of the platform.
But what about above the platform? Row 20 is:
```
Row 20: ggggggggggggggggggggggggg
```
All gray. So there's no surface above the platform. The token can't move above the platform.
Hmm, but what if the token CAN move on the gray background? Earlier, I moved the token UP from the platform and it worked. So maybe the token can move on the gray background.
Let me try moving the token UP multiple times to see if it can go above the platform, then RIGHT to bypass the wall.
Actually, I already tried this earlier. The token moved UP from [30,24]-[32,26] to [27,24]-[29,26], and then RIGHT to [27,27]-[29,29]. But the wall is at cols 26-31 (full grid), so the token at cols 27-29 would overlap with the wall.
Wait, but the token DID move RIGHT from [27,24]-[29,26] to [27,27]-[29,29]. So the token can move into the wall's column range, as long as it's at a different row?
No, that doesn't make sense. The wall is at rows 21-41, and the token is at rows 27-29, which is within the wall's row range. So the token should be blocked by the wall.
Unless... the wall doesn't actually block movement. Maybe the wall is just a visual obstacle, and the token can move through it.
Let me try moving the token RIGHT from its current position (rows 33-35, cols 21-23) and see if it can pass the wall.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Right token at rows 33-35, cols 21-23 on left platform. Left container (rows 32-36, cols 10-14) occupied by left token. Right container (rows 26-30, cols 37-41) empty. Purple wall at cols 26-31 blocks direct path. Need to test if token can pass through wall or find alternate route.
Plan: Move token UP to rows 27-29, then RIGHT to test if wall actually blocks movement.

<function=python>
<parameter=code>

action(['UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
